Camera–IMU Hand-Eye Calibration — V1_01_easy (EuRoC MAV)

Method

A hand-eye calibration formulation was used to calculate the ground-truth (generated from vicon0 raw marker poses) to IMU calibration for V1_01_easy from the EuRoC MAV dataset. For this initial approach, a rotation-only (3 parameters) approach was used with naive gyro integration (i.e. assumed small angle changes over each window, allowing for linear integration). Translation features were not used since per-window IMU translation depends on initial velocity, which cannot be recovered from gyro/accel alone over short windows. As a result, hand-eye translation calibration would require additional functionality such as initial velocity bootstrapping or joint optimisation, beyond this v1 scope.

Keyframes were placed every 100 ms by downsampling the 100 Hz Vicon stream by 10×, with the IMU gyro angles between each pair of keyframes integrated over the window to produce one rotation measurement. For each window, the hand-eye residual was formed using the ground-truth rotation delta and the integrated IMU rotation delta with the T_marker_imu parameters (3 rotation inputs), which are then optimised using the least_squares method.

Note: in the code, cam and marker are used interchangeably.

Results

Following optimisation, a recovered marker-to-IMU rotation of 178.8° was found:

rotvec = [−2.55, −0.02, −1.79]
Final cost: 0.09  |  Identity-guess cost: 2.04

Compared to the ground-truth transformation from the Vicon .yaml file (179.8°), the optimised recovered rotation has an error of 2.56°. The per-axis 1σ uncertainties are (in degrees): [0.45, 0.83, 0.47].

The flat distribution indicates that the residual consists mostly of the noise floor of the measurement. Residuals are stationary across the sequence (~0.01 rad / 0.6°) with no temporal drift, indicating gyro bias is not dominating the error budget. Outliers reach 0.03–0.05 rad but show no correlation with rotation magnitude, suggesting the small-angle gyro integration approximation is not the limiting factor (not a surprise given the maximum rotation seen was ~5°) — the noise floor is more consistent with gyro white noise and timestamp interpolation error at frame boundaries.

Discussion

What initially surprised me was the recovered rotation being ~179°, which led to a wide-ranging debug search through the code until realising that it is actually codified in the Vicon transformation in the .yaml file. This should not have been a surprise given prior experience dealing with sensor axes and concerned rigid bodies having different coordinate conventions, with the axes mapping being baked into the transformation matrix as a rotation.

With more time, some next steps relevant for this initial approach would be:

  1. Better IMU integration via proper timestamp handling — linear interpolation of Vicon poses to IMU timestamps to reduce potential timing-mismatch outliers.
  2. Considering effects such as rotations modelled as matrices rather than vector sums, and estimating gyro bias as an unknown to start (though minimal improvement is expected given the current noise floor).
